file_name: Attallah and Sharkas - 2021 - Intelligent dermatologist tool for classifying multiple skin cancer subtypes by incorporating manifo.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes an intelligent dermatologist tool for classifying multiple skin cancer subtypes using AI techniques. Specifically, it incorporates high-level deep learning features from ResNet-50, DenseNet-201, and DarkNet-53, along with low-level features from discrete wavelet transform (DWT) and local binary pattern (LBP), combined with machine learning classifiers such as SVM and KNN to achieve classification results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' in the header. It presents original experimental work proposing an 'intelligent dermatologist tool' for classifying skin cancer subtypes. The paper details specific methods (incorporating manifold radiomics features from ResNet-50, DenseNet-201, DarkNet-53, DWT, and LBP), describes the dataset used (HAM1000), and reports original quantitative results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score) comparing the proposed tool against related works, which are characteristics of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Intelligent Dermatologist Tool for Classifying Multiple Skin Cancer Subtypes by Incorporating Manifold Radiomics Features Categories
brief_summary: This study proposes an intelligent dermatologist tool for the automatic classification of multiple skin cancer subtypes using an integration of artificial intelligence and manifold radiomics features. The method incorporates high-level deep learning features from ResNet-50, DenseNet-201, and DarkNet-53, combined with low-level features from discrete wavelet transform (DWT) and local binary pattern (LBP). The model was evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, which includes seven skin lesion categories. The proposed tool utilizes a cubic Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier to achieve superior performance compared to related works. The results demonstrated an accuracy of 99%, with a sensitivity of 98.54%, specificity of 99.69%, and F1-score of 98.83%. These findings indicate that merging manifold features of different categories significantly enhances classification accuracy for skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven skin cancer categories classified in the HAM10000 dataset. Classified as a nonmelanocytic benign lesion in the introduction, but included as a target class in the experiment.

all_task_types.target: Seven skin cancer subtypes (ak, bcc, blk, df, mel, nv, vasc)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes an intelligent dermatologist tool for the automatic classification of multiple skin cancer subtypes. It uses a cubic Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier to predict discrete diagnostic categories from the HAM10000 dataset. The task is explicitly described as 'classifying multiple skin cancer subtypes' and 'multiclass classification' (7 classes). Performance metrics reported include accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1-score, which are standard for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing 10,008 dermoscopic photos of skin lesions, used for the automatic classification of seven skin cancer subtypes.

use.training: Used to train three CNNs (ResNet-50, DenseNet-201, DarkNet-53) via transfer learning and to extract high-level features from the last average pooling layer.
use.validation: Used for validation during CNN training (validation frequency set to 20).
use.testing: Used to evaluate the proposed intelligent dermatologist tool's classification performance (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score).
use.other: Images were resized and augmented prior to feature extraction.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To visualize and calculate per-class performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score) for the cubic SVM classifier.
To evaluate the diagnostic ability of the quadratic SVM classifier by plotting the True Positive Rate against the False Positive Rate.
To quantify the overall performance of the classifier, with reported values of 1.00 for specific classes (blk, df, nv, vasc) in the quadratic SVM model.

statistical_methods_used.method: mRMR (Minimum Redundancy Maximum Relevance) Feature Selection
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To select the best radiomics features for classification, resulting in improved accuracy (99.1% for linear, 99% for quadratic, 98.8% for cubic SVM).
To measure the proportion of correct predictions among the total number of cases, used to compare different feature combinations and classifiers.
To provide detailed performance metrics for each of the seven skin cancer subtypes and the mean performance of the proposed tool.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using classification accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score, confusion matrices, and ROC curves/AUC.

best_model_performance.model: Cubic SVM with ResNet-50 + DenseNet-201 + DarkNet-53 + DWT-A + LBP features

architecture_used.main_model: Integration of three Convolutional Neural Networks (ResNet-50, DenseNet-201, DarkNet-53) for feature extraction, combined with a cubic Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier for final classification.
architecture_used.pretraining: The three CNNs were pretrained on the ImageNet dataset using transfer learning.
architecture_used.input_size: Images were resized and augmented prior to feature extraction; specific input dimensions are not reported.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to specific dimensions for each CNN (224 × 224 × 3 for ResNet-50 and DenseNet-201; 256 × 256 × 3 for DarkNet-53)
Data augmentation to balance the unbalanced dataset, including shearing, rotation, and top and bottom hat filtering

conclusions: Merging manifold radiomics features from different categories (high-level deep learning features and low-level handcrafted features) significantly enhances classification accuracy for skin cancer subtypes.

The proposed intelligent dermatologist tool, which integrates high-level features from ResNet-50, DenseNet-201, and DarkNet-53 with low-level features from Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) and Local Binary Pattern (LBP), achieved superior performance compared to related AI-based tools.

The best-performing model (cubic SVM with fused features) achieved an accuracy of 99%, a mean sensitivity of 98.54%, a mean specificity of 99.69%, and a mean F1-score of 98.83% on the HAM10000 dataset.

Using low-level features alone (DWT and LBP) is insufficient for accurate skin cancer classification, yielding accuracies between 33.5% and 70.5%, whereas high-level deep learning features provide higher baseline accuracy.
The integration of high-level and low-level features improves classification performance more than using either feature type individually.

The proposed tool can assist dermatologists in accurate diagnosis, overcome limitations of manual diagnosis, reduce diagnostic errors, and potentially enhance patient survival rates by enabling early detection.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., 6705 nevus vs. 115 dermatofibroma cases), potentially biasing the model towards majority classes.

The paper explicitly acknowledges a major limitation: the use of 5-fold cross-validation for validation is insufficient for clinical deployment; cross-center validation using external datasets is required but not performed.

Performance metrics for rare classes (dermatofibroma and vascular lesion) are reported as perfect (100% sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score), which may indicate overfitting or data leakage given the small sample sizes (115 and 142 images respectively).

The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (p-values) to compare the proposed tool's performance against related works, relying solely on point estimates of accuracy and other metrics.

The dataset was augmented (shearing, rotation, filtering) to balance classes, but the specific impact of this augmentation on the generalizability of the low-level feature extraction (DWT/LBP) is not discussed.

The proposed tool's superiority is claimed based on comparisons with related works, but the specific preprocessing and feature extraction pipelines of those comparative studies are not standardized or controlled for in this paper.
